speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, this is tape 3, interviewing Malka Baran. Is there's something that we-- uh, that you would like to mention that we didn't cover in the previous tape that comes to your mind or to your memory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think I'll continue a little bit about that. So whatever I want to mention is just the beginning. Uh, with the chain that I sold for bread, I couldn't bring myself to sell the heart because it was the only thing from my parents. Now, in the camp, after being shocked and after losing my m-- memory for the period of time that I lost and didn't recall anything from that, I don't recall thinking about my parents in the camp. I also don't recall continuing that evening pros-- procedure with God, uh, in any way in the camp. Uh, I probably-- what I try to ex-- explain to myself now. I was probably shocked and let the inner part of me to sort of go to sleep. And everything that was happening, uh, with me then in camp was like out-- outer, flowing out on the outside of me and not so much inside. Inside was sort of numb and put away. Uh, that's why I don't recall thinking about my parents. I recall working. We worked many, many hours during the day. I was like a automaton, you know, doing what I have to do. But I remember singing to a child. We, uh, had a very strange and miraculous incident in my barrack. Uh, very early at our imprisonment, there was a child, a very young child thrown into our barrack. Uh, and we hid him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was this child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe two and a half. He was hidden in a bundle. We heard him. I wrote a story about it. We heard cry of a baby. And I thought that I'm going mad, because we knew that all babies were killed or destroyed or nonexistent. But here it was. And we-- a woman got up-- it was at night-- and slowly went toward the voice of the child and brought back a bundle with a child. It turned out later-- then, a day later, a woman came to our barrack as a new prisoner. This was his mother, because he stretched his arms to her. And she tried to deny it, not knowing that we'll not give him in. Uh, that child stayed in our barrack hidden. During the daytime-- during morning, when the Germans took us out to count, they didn't usually enter the barracks. They just called, and we came out. The child knew that he has to hide under the boards and be quiet. During the day, his mother remained in the barrack to clean. And he remained with her. When I came from work-- I always loved children. I told you before. He was really my savior. I played with him. I sang to him all the songs that I knew-- in the camp, in that terrible, terrible situation. And I told him stories. And so it was until the end. He was saved with us together. He survived with his mother. What we were doing-- working many, many hours, coming back exhausted. There was no bathroom to wash. There was no preparing for bed, because we didn't change our clothes. You can imagine how we-- what condition we were in. But we washed ourselves with snow. There was, um, a tank where the shells were washed. So I remember when I wanted to wash my one and only shirt, uh, from my body, we used to change with another girl. And I used to sit-- put it in that tank and rinse it and, uh-- and-- and dry it where the-- where the ammunition was dried, where the shells were dried. And put it on while she was waiting in a little room-- at work was a bathroom-- uh, until I'm ready. I put it on. She used to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's the only way we washed it, and very seldom. In the snow outside, we could wash our faces and bodies. Uh, conditions were very bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Food that we got was one piece of bread-- I don't know how many grams-- which was supposed to last us for three portions a day. For lunch we had, uh, soup made of potato skin with water. And for at night, we got some Ersatzkaffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, that's a substitute for coffee, some kind of dark liquid. And that's what we lived on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was with the coffee? Did you eat anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were supposed to have that piece of bread that we were given a ration. But some of us could not hold it till the end. After the war-- I was very sick in camp too, very sick. I contracted some kind of a disease of the skin, where the whole skin was in-- in furun-- furuncles. Um, wounds with pus erupting from me everywhere-- on my breasts, on my feet, on my body. And it was itching terrible. It was like, uh, a itching disease that was contracted from somebody else and from dirt. Two girls-- I was the youngest in camp. Another one was young too-- 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES] How old were other women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other women were a little bit older. My friends with whom I made a very deep friendship in-- in the labor camp were maybe a year or two older than I was. And then the rest were in their 30s. No old women and no young children, of course, but we were laborers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So uh, when I was so very sick, I didn't care whether I live and-- or die. That other girl who was also sick with [POLISH], that was called in Polish-- I don't know how it is, uh-- itching-- contracting, uh, furuncle [POLISH]. Uh, she was sick too with the same disease. And the Germans took her out of the hall. And we were sure that they went to shoot her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a Jewish doctor who took care of the prisoners who were sick, uh, took me out of the hall, of the working labor hall, and put me in another barrack with sick people. I remember all around me were typhus people-- uh, very sick. And he tried to save them. They bandaged me from top to toe with all those wounds and, uh, injected some-- he found a vein, obviously, from my body and injected into other parts of the body. And I was there for a long time healing. I was extremely passive. I really couldn't care if I die or live. I think I didn't think of my parents because I expected to die and join them or whatever. We didn't expect to be-- to surviving. Um, I was there. And of course, they cut my hair short because I contracted typhoid after that. And all that is miraculous because I-- I was undernourished and shocked. And-- and-- and I survived both. When I got better, I went back to labor camp, to labor hall. And that was an incident.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was a barrack? How many women were there in a barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the exact numbers. But there were 3 times 16 on one. And there were maybe 12 of us. So must be a few hundred people, like 300.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these mostly Jewish woman? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish women. There were no other prisoners that came and stayed there. There were Polish workers who came during the day, worked a few hours and went home. We were all prisoners, Jewish prisoners. We were not paid, of course, not-- uh, and treated in a way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have some contact with the Polish workers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the one that worked with us at the machine-- Muszka-- that gave me that, uh, bread for the heart-- for the chain. And she used to bring us other things, very little, small object, because she was afraid. She was a very small, tiny woman-- came to work, was afraid. They were not supposed to carry anything with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe, uh, what this camp looked like? Were there barbed wires? How-- uh, what were the surroundings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the wires. We were never-- I never went there. But I know we were enclosed. I don't know if they were electrical wires or any such thing. Uh, I-- I don't know. My friends probably know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, how many hours did you work? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: From early morning till late at night. There were shifts. There was a night shift and a day shift. So it's probably 12 hours-- day shift-- and sometimes at night. We switched.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you wearing specific uniforms in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just my clothes. And we wore numbers on the clothes. At the end of the labor-- uh, at the end-- before the end of the war-- of course, we didn't know that was the end of the war-- no radios, no newspapers. They tried to-- they were talked about putting numbers on our hands. But they never accomplished that. It was too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were done with work, uh, and you were together, back with them, what-- what-- did you discuss-- did you discuss anything among ourselves? Uh, what did you talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: We probably talked about, uh, daily things. But I don't remember. We were exhausted. We usually went to sleep to prepare for-- to-- to sleep as much as we could with bugs and everything else to get ready for the next day. There was not much. But we developed very close friendship, mostly through telling stories to the child, uh, singing some songs, talking to one another. I don't recall anything else. But we are very close friends. And those that survived are now in Israel. And we meet every year. And we have close relationship with them. And interestingly, they talk about the same camp, the same situation, and they remember things that I don't remember. I remember things that they don't remember. So the memory is very, um, fractioned and selective for each one of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you dream at night when you were asleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I was grateful that I don't know. Do you know now when you dream? You don't know. Sometimes, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long-- how long were you in this-- in this camp? Until--

speaker: interviewee
text: Till '45, January.

speaker: interviewer
text: January of '45?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. So it was, uh, from March '43 to January '45-- I guess two and a half years, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, uh, you continued with this ammunition work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did liberation come to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I remember is a very vivid picture of the working hall where we worked. All of a sudden, one morning, the German women, those two, three supervisors, overseers left the hall, which was unusual-- all of them-- and left us alone and didn't come back. The office, um, person, that American officer, left and didn't come back. We were alone. And we were so conditioned that we didn't even try to budge or move, even though we were alone, not supervised, until a young Jewish man ran into the hall and shouted, you are free! Go out! The Germans are running away! That was the end of-- and we still didn't budge. We-- we were so conditioned for two and half years. And nobody believed-- well, I at least, did not think that something like this could happen. But he-- uh, people-- somebody went to the door, pushed through. And then all of us went through the door and walked out of that factory, of that hall, through the camp, and out to the city. And when we entered the city-- because it was on the outskirts-- uh, we heard some shooting. We saw some tanks. We saw some Russian soldiers in the tanks-- some of them wounded, some of them not-- running. And, uh, the vehicles were going fast. And-- and we realized that we are in the midst of taking over. The Russian Army came and liberated Czestochowa. We ran-- because they were shooting still. We ran into-- my friends and I. Like, four girls with one mother, the mother of the child-- no--

speaker: interviewer
text: With the child with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Another mother. Another mother who survived, a young mother with a daughter. We ran into a building. We went into the basement. And we hid there until the shooting stopped. Uh, when we heard it's quiet in the morning, we walked-- looked out. It was peaceful. The Russian sol-- soldiers were, uh, drive-- riding in the city. Um, we investigated that building. We found an empty apartment. Obviously, it was formerly a Jewish apartment, taken over by Germans, now freed completely, because the Germans ran away. And we stayed in that apartment for a time. And what happened next that I remember-- that there organized itself a Jewish Gemeinde, a Jewish sort of organization that took care-- uh, began to take care of the survivors. And they sent us to various places of work. One of my friend and I, with my coat on, worked in a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, Hanka. We worked in a hospital, uh, peeling potatoes for the kitchen. We took some potatoes in our sleeves-- in my sleeves of the coat and brought them home to that apartment. Meantime, Gena, one of the girls, remained in the apartment, cleaned it up a little bit, found a pail, fairly clean. She cooked those potatoes every day, the pail of potatoes. And we ate it as though it was the most delicious treat every day. And we got-- we were swollen a little bit. We got swollen more. Uh, but my wounds healed before yet, after the treatment of the doctors. But I had stains all over my body. And so we worked in that hospital for a while. Then we were-- uh, the-- the organization suggested to us that if we want to study, go back to school, we can enter-- they organized-- they arranged a high school, a regular Polish high school for us to enter. So Hanka and I, the two who worked in the hospital, went to that school. We lasted there one day exactly, because I remember when we came with our torn, worn-out clothes, and the Polish girls looked at us, and the-- the-- the relationship-- not that there was any relationship there. Their, uh, welcoming was not very welcoming.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we didn't want to go back there for some reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were-- how was it not-- in what-- --in what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly what happened. But we did not feel safe or comfortable there. Maybe it was part of us. Maybe it was part of their, you know, looking at us with strange faces, and uh, maybe some negative statements. I don't recall. But I know that we didn't want to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we continued working in various places. And we met-- in that same building that we stayed, uh, we met a Russian Jewish soldier. He was not exactly a soldier. But it was in-- in an army uniform. He was-- uh, we got to know him through my friend. My friend Shoshanka, who now is in Israel, um, started talking to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was a Jew from Leningrad who had a wife and two daughters. And when he found out-- and he worked in the back of Russian Army. He was not in the front. He was doing repairs on the watch m-- watches of the soldiers--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out--

speaker: interviewee
text: --a watchmaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out that he was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw that he was Jewish, and he told us he is Jewish. And he was-- took very great interest in us. He began to bring-- and they ate in that, uh, building. There was a big restaurant where they cook for the soldiers. Uh, his garrison or part of the army was stationed there. So he brought us every day bits of food from the-- from the-- the lunchroom-- a little bread, a little rolls, some bits of meat. And so he wanted very much to help us. Then he had an idea. He was very simple, naive, uh, good-hearted Jewish fellow. He had an idea that he wants to save one of us. And he chose me. And, go after the war to return with me to Leningrad to have a family. We had a meeting with all my friends who were older than I am. And sort of-- I was still very passive. And they decided it's a wonderful idea. I don't have anyone. Everybody's killed. I'll have a family. But uh-- but soon we saw-- he came and said that his part of the army is moved-- that was still war in other parts of the-- Czestochowa was liberated. But the army was moving toward Germany and Austria, or Austria and Germany, in that order-- Germany and Austria. And he was removed from that-- from Czestochowa with his soldiers-- uh, with the part of the army that he served. And he said goodbye, and we remained. After some time, he sent a lieutenant with a note. Follow him. He will bring you to us-- to me. And he-- the note was for me. Uh, the girls decided that I should go, but not alone-- that I should go with another girl. One of them, Shoshanka, volunteered. I'm going with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went, both of us, with that lieutenant. And we traveled on an open truck with 16 soldiers, Russian soldiers, on one truck. And the second one-- open truck with 16 soldiers on the other truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: We left in the morning. We were sure that we'll arrive at our destination toward the evening. We didn't even ask. We were completely out of normal kind of situation life. Somebody wanted us. Somebody wanted to adopt me. How wonderful. I'm going. She's going with me. We have nothing to keep us in Czestochowa. We have no one survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't go to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Um, we followed-- we went on the trucks. We traveled through destroyed cities, through Germany until the evening. The evening came. They stopped at the abandoned village-- no houses erect, broken windows everywhere, not a soul to be seen.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was only, um-- where do you keep cows and horses? A s-- stable. S--

speaker: interviewee
text: A stable with a broken, open door. They stopped there. It was evening. Uh, that was around March. It was sort of not very cold. And uh, they set up a camp, made fires, started cooking. And the lieutenant comes to us, to the truck. And we stayed on the truck. And Shoshanka says to me, uh-oh, that begins to be dangerous. We are alone. We are not budging from the truck. We stay here. Remember not to move. She was a very active kind of a person, took, uh, charge of everything. And I followed, of course. And the lieutenant comes to us and says, come down. We have soup. You warm up. You eat a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: We said, no, thank you. We'll stay here. This was a Russian lieutenant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the one that brought the note. And-- and that other man-- Isidor was his name-- that also reminded me of my father. He had freckles. He had yellow hair. And he was sort of an elderly-- not elderly, but in his 30s. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this lieutenant know that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Probably. Anyway, he took very good care of us. But we didn't come down. So he brought us soup to the truck. And we ate that soup. Meantime, I remember the picture. The soldiers began to sing. And they had harmonicas, uh, accordions. And they sang. And there was music. And we were cuddled together. And it began to drizzle. And it began to be colder. And the lieutenant begs us, come down, warm up. No, we stay on the truck. He brought us blankets to cover ourselves. And we stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Soon enough, the music stops. The fire was out. They cleaned the dishes-- whatever they had, those-- uh, and they walk into that sta-- uh, stable? Farm, um, thing. And they lie-- they went to sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the lieutenant comes to us and says, look, girls, it's very cold. And you cannot stay on the truck. You'll just freeze. I made room for you near the door. The door is open. I'm responsible for you. Come down. I put some soft things on it. Lie down and rest. Shoshanka said no. We stayed there and stayed there. And finally she said, we really cannot stay here. Let's go down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we walked down. And we lie down near the opening, a very nice place-- nice-- on the floor, but prepared. And we laid down. And she said, don't you dare to fall asleep. All around us, the soldiers are sleeping, [POLISH], snoring.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INTERVIEWER LAUGHS] And we are terrified. And don't you dare to fall asleep. I wrote a story about it that-- it was-- I called it 'The Tiny Flame.' I'll tell you why. Anyway, of course, we were exhausted. So we both fell asleep. In the middle of the night, we both jolted. Something fell on us. And at first, my heart was beating. And-- and I heard hers. And we were terrified. And we were numb. And we were frozen. And it was quiet. It was nothing-- p-- peace and quiet. And we sort of relaxed. Our hearts stopped, uh, race-- racing. And we fell asleep. In the morning, we get up. And a blanket of down is covering us. And I describe that moment-- [SOBBING] I still cry when I talk about it. [CRYING] [DRINKS] It was so-- you must understand that for-- for five-- four years, we didn't hear a human kindness or experience a human kindness. [SOBBING] So in the first occurrence of somebody who was kind to us in-- in such a way that could have been a disaster-- meant so much. I just felt like balloons exploded in the air. And it was so beautiful. And we ate breakfast with them. And we sang with them. And we followed with them the second day. And toward the evening of the second day, we arrived at that Isidor place. And of course, we started working for the Russian Army, in the back of the Army, terre fronte.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES] What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Which means, what did-- what did we work with? I was a-- a shepherdess. I was running after the cows. The Russian Army was carrying in the, uh, wagons, in the, uh, trains horses and cows and sheep. That was called [NON-ENGLISH], um, housekeeping for the Army. And every step they made, uh, we were working with them. I tried to milk the cow. I wasn't successful. I tried to be a shepherdess. I wasn't successful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put me in the kitchen. I washed the floors. I served the dishes. I washed the dishes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they put us in the photography. I learned to develop pictures. And I worked in the photography.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of food did you eat there? What-- what kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, delicious food, good food--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: --for the Russian Army. Russian, uh, kra-- uh, cabbage soup with meat. Um, delicious soup. We were just going back to-- to food that kept us alive later.

speaker: interviewer
text: You ate three meals a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three meals a day, good food in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things you have for breakfast?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. But it was delicious. Probably normal Russian, good food. Uh-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] --that was until--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your--

speaker: interviewee
text: --J-- uh, May '45, when the war ended officially. We were, at that moment-- I was working in the kitchen, not yet in photography. And we were standing outside under the tree. And the soldiers were celebrating. Oh, I forgot a very important moment. I have to go back for a second.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The liberation moment, when we walked out, and we found ourselves in the basement, this is the first time that I cried. Through all the years of camp and the ghetto, that was the first time that I cried, because I realized that I'm left alone. That was the time when I thought about my family. I remember distinctly. I am survivor. I am alive. It's after the war. But I have no one. All of them died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knew that I expected to die too. And that was a terrifying moment. And that was the first time I cried from pain that they are not with me. This was when the Russian Army came to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when we left the-- the-- the labor camp and we found ourselves free in the basement, that's when I started crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're coming to the end of the tape. We'll continue, because I wanted to ask you--
